We have 4 major events that can cause chaos for the markets. 

1) SPCX IPO -6 trading sessions leading up to this we saw low volume and liquidity drain from other stocks/holdings.   
2) Anthropic IPO  
3) OpenAI IPO  
4) Elec in November

I think the market could drop at a baseline 30%. Especially in the concentrated holdings. 

But beyond that I think we can see a swan dive once people realize they can’t liquidate their entire holdings at the current price. $75T value vs $22.7T in circulation. 

Means many retirement accounts are paper gains only. 

Credit markets are stagnating due to treasuries remaining sticky. Banks are going to be offsetting this with higher fixed rates so businesses continue herding into variable rates. 

Consumer HH savings is at an all time low. 

There is a lot more going on under the hood than deciding whether they tank the USD or tank the market. 

In fact tanking USD may create a situation of bringing jobs back home.

At the same time amat, mu and sndk all said that they have contractually guaranteed orders for the next 5 years and that they are only investing into production capacity what enabels them to satisfy that backlog, basically trying to avoid the typical cycal revenue margin crush. If we follow Aschenbrenner's thesis further we can assume that current investment's should be sufficient to train the next generation of models and the next orders of magnitude, as he calls it, of capability for generic models will be primarily driven by algorhitmic optimization itself.

top_bearish_posts.text:
Wall Street thought memory price hikes would flatten out in Q3, but the brand-new trade data from Taiwan and South Korea proves they completely underestimated the market.

What Wall Street expected: Analysts predicted standard Server DRAM and PC RAM price hikes would slow down to just 3% to 13% for Q3.

South Korea (Data out June 14): Memory semiconductor export values exploded by 254.9% year-on-year, and enterprise AI SSD storage shipments surged 337.7%. Because factory capacity is completely fixed, this triple-digit value spike proves Q3 contract prices are rising way faster than expected.

—————————  
—————————

Taiwan’s "Outlandish" Export Numbers (Released June 9, 2026)

The Peak: Taiwan’s exports surged 51.7% year-on-year (crushing the market consensus of 41.2%). 

The Pricing Index: Taiwan’s official export price index rose 18.1%, driven almost entirely by semiconductors.

The Upstream Hike: Because supply is so choked, TSMC just confirmed it is moderately raising foundry prices for the second half of the year, with advanced 3nm nodes seeing up to a 15% hike due to soaring raw material and substrate costs.

Wall Street expecting $34.4 Billion  
But looks like it be around \~

top_bearish_posts.text: Inflation is due to increase in oil prices.  With deal, oil flows (eventually) and cost of goods should go down  (transportation costs, grow costs, manufacturing costs, etc) and inflation will go back towards target.  Will take some time, but that’s the hope from a deal…inflation gets back under control, Feds can cut rates, long term rates will go down, people in debt will slow their debt increase and people that have money will borrow money to buy more things…

As someone who used Adobe products professionally and casually, and took classes on using them, they're way too fucking expensive. The cost should come down over time. They're now just a middle man between the work and the people hired to do it. 

They doubled my prices. They have a long history of shady tactics to keep you subscribed, and it honestly makes me bitter when I don't use it for two months but am still paying the subscription fee. I cancelled and I've been a customer for probably 15 years in some capacity.

AI should be making Adobe products more affordable. There are plenty of free alternatives. Instead they're relying on licensing and the cultural reliance on their products to keep themselves going. It's not a good model for investing unless they can figure out a better pricing model, at least for me and I'm probably not their target customer anymore.

IDK.  They're already using ram built into cpu in laptops.  Once they start making those for desktops people won't need separate ram, which may increase the cost since it'll be scarce but it also won't sell as much.  Newer CPUs and GPUs are where its at.  Plus part of the current ram price frenzy is because they're selling most of them for AI, reducing public availability intentionally, but sooner than later AI won't require as much energy either and the ram requirements may reduce too leading to a flood in the market of available ram.  I think that's part of why they're controlling it so tightly right now is to try and prevent that flood.

This is a massive misconception around what happened the last few weeks. The SpaceX IPO had next to zero impact on liquidity flow. Yes the market cap broke $2t, but the IPO was not for $2t. The active float is 5%. The offering was $75b. If every single dollar was funded via selling nvda it would represent a sell off of 1.5%, of literally just NVDA; or 0.33% of the Mag7. This sell off to rotate into the IPOs is misguided and not at all what we saw last week. OpenAi and Anthropic will each likely offer 5-10% of float at offering, assuming both are valued at around $1.25T, you’re talking maybe $250b, 1% of mag7, of liquidity required until lockup periods end.

What we actually saw was much simpler. We had a surprisingly strong jobs print. In an elevated inflation environment, a weak job market is what kept the idea of rate cuts realistic. With strong jobs growth, rate cut probability dropped dramatically and the market stalled along with it as it puts direct pressure on EPS growth estimates and in turn forward PE. This is why there’s a resurgence in the peace talks leading into the FOMC meeting on the 17th. Without a peace deal in place which Warsh can deliver along with his adjustmen
